notes.anchor: acting as auctioneers are forbidden to sit among the decurions
notes.note: A recent lex (probably Caesarian) barred those currently practising as praecones (public criers / auctioneers) from holding a seat on the local senate (ordo decurionum) of a municipium; the relief Cicero reports is that the ban was prospective only and did not touch those who had formerly done so. Praecones were considered a disreputable trade, associated with the slave-market and public sales.

notes.anchor: men who today practise haruspicy are being enrolled into the Senate at Rome
notes.note: Haruspices were Etruscan-trained diviners who read omens from entrails; regarded as a lower-grade religious function than augury and socially marginal. Cicero's jest is that Caesar was enrolling such men in the Roman Senate while a far lesser stigma barred respectable municipal men from town councils.

Gnaeus Pompeius the Younger held the Pompeian remnant in Spain; Cicero relays intelligence from Caesar's own dispatch, citing a letter of Paciaecus, a Spanish local notable, reporting eleven legions — a formidably large force, the background to the Munda campaign of March 45 BC.

Lepta and Galba were co-sureties (sponsores) for a debt owed by Gnaeus Pompeius the Younger; with Pompeius now a defeated enemy in Spain the recovery of anything was precarious, and Cicero promises to consult the co-surety Galba about what can be salvaged.

Cicero's Orator (46 BC), his third major treatise on the ideal orator, dedicated to Brutus; it sets out his defence of the grand Asianic style against the Atticists. Cicero here hopes the young son of Lepta is old enough to profit from reading it.

A quotation from Hesiod, Works and Days 289 (the full line: 'but in front of excellence the immortal gods have placed sweat'), the passage urging toil as the path to virtue. Cicero is gently suggesting the boy apply himself to demanding texts.
